title: Doubly Linked List
description: Adding a prev pointer to enable backward traversal — the exact structure behind STL list's bidirectional iterators, plus its memory trade-off.
objectives: Add a prev pointer to enable backward traversal
Traverse a doubly linked list in both directions
Know the memory trade-off compared to a singly linked list

sections.text: 1. Recap: Singly Only Goes One Way

Singly Linked List's nodes only knew their next neighbor — no way to go backward. This is exactly why Iterators described list's iterators as bidirectional, unlike forward_list's forward-only ones. A doubly linked list is what makes that backward movement possible.
2. The Node Now Has Two Pointers
A doubly linked list is a linked list where each node stores pointers to both its next and previous neighbors, allowing traversal in either direction.
3. Full Example: Insert and Traverse Both Ways

tail is now tracked too — with prev links, walking backward from tail is just as natural as walking forward from head. This is exactly what powers rbegin()/rend() from Iterators, previously used on vector — list's version works the same way, using prev instead of pointer arithmetic.

Recall Singly Linked List's deletion: it had to walk from head, specifically tracking the node BEFORE the target, since a singly linked node has no way to look backward on its own. With prev, a node can find its own predecessor directly — nodeToDelete->prev — no separate search needed at all, given just a pointer to the node itself.
5. The Trade-off: Extra Memory Per Node

Recall forward_list's motivation: minimizing memory by storing only one link per node. A doubly linked list is the opposite trade-off — an extra pointer (prev) per node costs real memory (recall sizeof from Memory Addresses), in exchange for bidirectional traversal and simpler deletion. Neither choice is universally correct; it depends on whether that extra memory or that extra capability matters more for the problem at hand.

sections.title: A scavenger hunt where every box also points back to the previous one
sections.text: Recall the one-way scavenger hunt from Singly Linked List. A doubly linked list is that same hunt, except every box also has a second note pointing back to whichever box sent you there — letting you retrace your steps at any point, at the cost of every box now needing to carry two notes instead of one.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to update BOTH next and prev when inserting or deleting a node — a doubly linked list requires keeping both directions consistent.
Forgetting to update tail when inserting at the end, or head when inserting/deleting at the front.
Assuming a doubly linked list is always better than singly linked — it costs extra memory per node, which matters when memory is tight.
Deleting a node without first saving pointers to its neighbors — updating nodeToDelete->next before reading nodeToDelete->prev (or vice versa) in the wrong order can lose the reference needed.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the key structural difference between a singly and doubly linked list?
questions.answer: A doubly linked list's nodes store an additional prev pointer to the previous node, alongside next, enabling traversal in both directions.

questions.question: Why does a prev pointer simplify deleting a specific node?
questions.answer: Because the node can find its own predecessor directly via prev, without needing a separate traversal from the head to locate the node just before it, as a singly linked list requires.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of using a doubly linked list instead of a singly linked list?
questions.answer: Extra memory per node for the additional prev pointer, in exchange for bidirectional traversal and simpler deletion given just a pointer to the target node.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A doubly linked list adds a prev pointer to each node alongside next, enabling traversal in both directions — exactly the structure behind STL list's bidirectional iterators. This makes deleting a given node simpler: it can find its own predecessor directly through prev, without the separate 'walk from head to find the node before it' search a singly linked list requires. The cost is one extra pointer's worth of memory per node, the opposite trade-off from forward_list, which minimizes memory by keeping only a single link.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Doubly Linked List
sections.text: Doubly linked = every box has TWO notes, one pointing forward, one pointing back. Costs extra memory per box, buys you backward traversal and easy self-deletion.
LeetCode: Doubly Linked List in Practice

sections.title: Design Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Design your own linked list, supporting get(index), addAtHead(val), addAtTail(val), addAtIndex(index, val), and deleteAtIndex(index).

examples.input: addAtHead(1); addAtTail(3); addAtIndex(1,2); get(1); deleteAtIndex(1); get(1)

hints: This is exactly this lesson's DoublyLinkedList class, extended with indexed operations — walk from head OR tail, whichever is closer to the target index, using both next and prev.
addAtIndex/deleteAtIndex both need to find the node just before the target position first, then relink around it — the same pattern as insertAtEnd, generalized to any position.
Keeping both head AND tail pointers (like this lesson's class already does) makes addAtTail O(1) instead of requiring a full traversal.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use a plain vector internally to back the list operations — simple, but insertion/deletion in the middle costs O(n) for shifting, same as any array.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) per insert/delete due to shifting

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A genuine doubly linked list with head and tail pointers, exactly this lesson's structure, extended with indexed traversal.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) for head/tail ops, O(n) for arbitrary index traversal
